info.name: IncidentPilot — Monitoring Stack APIs
info.description:
Complete Postman collection for the IncidentPilot monitoring stack.

Base URLs:
  Flask Generator — http://localhost:5001
  Prometheus       — http://localhost:9090
  Loki             — http://localhost:3100
  Grafana          — http://localhost:3000 (admin / admin)
  Gradio UI        — http://localhost:7860 (browser only, no REST API)

Usage workflow:
  1. Start the stack: docker compose up -d
  2. Verify health with Flask /health → Prometheus /-/ready → Loki /ready → Grafana /api/health
  3. Trigger an incident: POST /api/incidents/pool/trigger
  4. Watch metrics change: GET /api/incidents/state (every 3s)
  5. Query Prometheus for the latest metric values
  6. Query Loki for application logs
  7. Open Grafana dashboards (http://localhost:3000, admin/admin, "Incidents" folder)

item.name: 🟢 1. Health Checks
item.description: Verify all 4 Docker services are running and responsive.

request.description: Returns the Flask generator's operational status and whether an incident is currently active.
Prometheus readiness endpoint. Returns 200 when Prometheus is ready to accept queries.
Loki readiness endpoint. Returns 200 when Loki is ready to accept logs and queries.
Grafana health check. Requires Basic Auth (admin / admin).

item.name: ⚡ 2. Incident Simulator (Flask)
item.description: Trigger, monitor, and resolve simulated production incidents in real-time.

request.description:
Start a Postgres connection-pool exhaustion scenario.

Lifecycle: climbing (15s) → plateau (15s) → recovering (10s) → auto-resolve

Metric behaviour:
  - active_connections climbs from 118 to 200
  - p99_latency rises from 380ms to ~1830ms
  - error_rate climbs from 0.05% to ~6.1%

Pool error logs ({"level": "ERROR", "message": "could not obtain connection..."}) appear when connections >= 190.

Start a Redis cache node failover scenario.

Lifecycle: failover (6s) → warming (12s) → auto-resolve

Metric behaviour:
  - cache_hit_ratio drops from 0.95 to 0.41 (step change)
  - p99_latency rises up to ~930ms (cache misses hit DB directly)
  - error_rate stays at BASELINE (0.05%) — requests still succeed, just slower

Key difference from pool: NO error spike during cache incidents.

Start a fraud-scoring-service outage scenario.

Lifecycle: active (20s) → auto-resolve

Metric behaviour:
  - error_rate spikes to 10-15% (all 503s from fraud-scoring-svc)
  - p99_latency jumps to ~836ms (2.2x baseline)
  - active_connections and cache_hit_ratio stay at baseline

Fraud error logs: {"level": "ERROR", "message": "fraud-scoring-svc unavailable"}
Start a randomly-selected scenario (pool, cache, or fraud). Useful for demo variety.

Force-resolve the active incident. Use the kind name (pool/cache/fraud) or 'current' to resolve whatever is running.

Returns:
  - {"status": "resolved", "kind": "pool", "phase": "resolved"} — when matched
  - {"status": "no_active_incident"} — when no incident is running
  - {"status": "no_active_incident", "expected": "cache", "active": "pool"} — kind mismatch

Convenience endpoint: force-resolve the active incident regardless of kind.

Returns:
  - {"status": "resolved", "kind": "pool", "phase": "resolved", "request_id": "..."} — when resolved
  - {"status": "no_active_incident", "request_id": "..."} — when no incident is running

Get the current incident state. Returns current metrics snapshot.

Active incident example:
{
  "kind": "pool",
  "phase": "climbing",
  "phase_progress": 0.5,
  "tick_count": 7,
  "p99_latency_ms": 1082.5,
  "error_rate_pct": 2.96,
  "active_connections": 158,
  "cache_hit_ratio": 0.951,
  "auto_resolve": true,
  "request_id": "a1b2c3d4e5f6"
}

No incident example:
{
  "kind": "none",
  "phase": "none",
  "phase_progress": 0,
  "tick_count": 0,
  "p99_latency_ms": 380.0,
  "error_rate_pct": 0.05,
  "active_connections": 118,
  "cache_hit_ratio": 0.95,
  "auto_resolve": true,
  "request_id": "a1b2c3d4e5f6"
}

Prometheus scrape endpoint in Prometheus plain-text format. This is what Prometheus consumes.

Example response line:
  checkout_p99_latency_ms{service="checkout-api"} 1486.2

item.name: 📊 3. Prometheus Queries
item.description: Query the Prometheus time-series database directly for metric values.

query.description: Start timestamp (Unix epoch seconds). Use Collection Variable: set in Pre-request Script.

request.description: Get the current (instant) value of a single metric. Useful for quick checks.

Check Prometheus scrape target status. Shows which endpoints Prometheus is scraping and their health.

Look for: target 'flask-generator:5000' with health='up'.

item.name: 📝 4. Loki Log Queries
item.description: Query the Loki log aggregation system for application logs.

query.description: LogQL stream selector — matches all logs from the checkout-api service

response.name: Example Loki response

request.description: Filter loki log lines to only those containing 'ERROR'. Useful for checking whether the pool exhaustion or fraud scenarios are firing error logs.

query.description: LogQL: filter for lines containing 'connection' (pool timeout errors)

request.description: Filter loki log lines for pool-connection timeout errors.

query.description: LogQL: filter for lines containing 'fraud' (fraud-scoring-svc unavailable errors)

request.description: Filter loki log lines for fraud-scoring-service unavailable errors.

item.name: 📈 5. Grafana Dashboard APIs
item.description: Grafana API endpoints for verifying dashboards and datasources.

request.description:
Search Grafana dashboards in the 'Incidents' folder. Returns all provisioned incident dashboards.

Expected dashboards:
  - Incident Overview
  - Pool Exhaustion
  - Cache Failover
  - Fraud Outage

url.description:
Get full dashboard definition for the Incident Overview dashboard. Replace 'incident-overview' with:
  - pool-exhaustion
  - cache-failover
  - fraud-outage

request.description:
Fetch the full dashboard JSON definition including panels, queries, and layout.

Available dashboard UIDs:
  - incident-overview
  - pool-exhaustion
  - cache-failover
  - fraud-outage
List all configured Grafana datasources. Should show both Prometheus and Loki.
Grafana home page. Opens the Grafana login/landing page in a browser.

item.name: 🚀 6. Incident Lifecycle Workflow
item.description: Complete end-to-end incident lifecycle: trigger → monitor → verify → resolve.

exec: console.log('Step 5: QUERY Prometheus for live metrics');
console.log('Step 6: QUERY Loki for application logs');

request.description:
This is a workflow guide, not an actual API call. Run the documented steps in order using the other requests in this collection.

See the script tab for the complete step-by-step instructions.

item.name: ▶️ Workflow: Compare Pool vs Cache vs Fraud

exec: console.log('Run each scenario separately and compare metrics:');
console.log('  Connections: climbs 118→200, pins at max');
console.log('  Logs:        ERROR "could not obtain connection from pool"');
console.log('  Latency:     climbs 380→930ms (step change at failover)');
console.log('  Error rate:  stays at 0.05% (NO errors)');
console.log('  Cache hit:   drops 0.95→0.41 step, then warms back');
console.log('  Logs:        WARN "Redis cluster failover detected"');
console.log('=== The key insight for the IncidentPilot agent ===');
console.log('Different scenarios have DISTINCT metric signatures:');
console.log('  High error rate + high latency + high connections = POOL');
console.log('  Low cache hit + high latency + normal errors     = CACHE');
console.log('  High error rate + no connection issue + normal    = FRAUD');

request.description: Comparison guide showing the distinct metric signatures of all 3 incident scenarios. This helps understand how the IncidentPilot agent distinguishes between them.
